title: Direct and indirect object pronouns
summary: COD and COI basics, how to tell them apart, and where they go in the sentence.

items.french: COD: me / te / le / la / nous / vous / les
items.english: Direct object pronouns: me / you / him-it / her-it / us / you / them
items.note: Use COD when the object answers who? or what? and receives the action directly.

items.french: COI: me / te / lui / nous / vous / leur
items.english: Indirect object pronouns: to me / to you / to him-her / to us / to you / to them
items.note: Use COI when the object answers to whom? or for whom? and often follows `à`.

items.french: Je vois Marie → Je la vois. / Je parle à Marie → Je lui parle.
items.english: I see Marie -> I see her. / I speak to Marie -> I speak to her.
items.note: Quick COD vs COI contrast: direct uses `la`, indirect uses `lui`.

items.french: Tu manges la pomme → Tu la manges. / Tu téléphones à tes parents → Tu leur téléphones.
items.english: You eat the apple -> You eat it. / You call your parents -> You call them.
items.note: The pronoun goes before the conjugated verb in the present tense.

items.french: J'ai acheté le livre → Je l'ai acheté. / J'ai envoyé un cadeau à Pierre → Je lui ai envoyé un cadeau.
items.english: I bought the book -> I bought it. / I sent a gift to Pierre -> I sent him a gift.
items.note: In passé composé, the pronoun still goes before the auxiliary.

items.french: Je vois qui ? Marie. → Je la vois. / Je parle à qui ? À Marie. → Je lui parle.
items.english: I see whom? Marie. -> I see her. / I speak to whom? To Marie. -> I speak to her.
items.note: Best cheat: ask the question first, then choose COD or COI.

items.french: Je le vois. / Je l'ai vu. / Je ne le vois pas. / Je ne lui parle pas.
items.english: I see him-it. / I saw him-it. / I do not see him-it. / I do not speak to him-her.
items.note: Position formulas: before the verb, before the auxiliary, and between `ne` and the verb in negation.

title: Demonstratives
summary: Demonstrative adjectives and pronouns for this, that, these, those, and the one.

items.french: ce / cet / cette / ces
items.english: this-that masculine / this-that before vowel-h / this-that feminine / these-those
items.note: Core demonstrative adjectives to memorize.

items.french: ce livre / cet homme / cette femme / ces livres / ces femmes
items.english: this-that book / this-that man / this-that woman / these-those books / these-those women
items.note: `ce` before consonant, `cet` before vowel or silent h, `cette` for feminine, `ces` for plural.

items.french: ce livre-ci / ce livre-là / cette maison-ci / cette maison-là / ces personnes-ci / ces personnes-là
items.english: this book here / that book there / this house here / that house there / these people here / those people there
items.note: Add `-ci` for near and `-là` for far. The suffix attaches to the noun, not the adjective.

items.french: celui / celle / ceux / celles
items.english: the one masculine / the one feminine / the ones masculine / the ones feminine
items.note: Use demonstrative pronouns when replacing a noun.

items.french: celui-ci / celui-là / celle-ci / celle-là
items.english: this one / that one / this one feminine / that one feminine
items.note: Add `-ci` and `-là` here too for contrast.

items.french: Je préfère celle-ci à celle-là. / celui qui parle / celle de Jean
items.english: I prefer this one to that one. / the one who is speaking / Jean's one
items.note: Demonstrative pronouns can be followed by `qui` or by `de + noun`.

items.french: Ma voiture est plus grande que celle de Jean.
items.english: My car is bigger than Jean's.
items.note: Common full-sentence example with a demonstrative pronoun.

title: Passé composé
summary: Main structure, negative form, regular participles, irregular participles, être agreement, and common exam verbs.

items.french: Sujet + auxiliaire (avoir ou être) + participe passé + complément
items.english: Subject + auxiliary (avoir or être) + past participle + complement
items.note: Base structure for le passé composé.

items.french: J'ai parlé. / Tu as fini. / Nous avons compris. / Je suis allé. / Elle est venue. / Ils sont partis.
items.english: I spoke. / You finished. / We understood. / I went. / She came. / They left.
items.note: Avoir verbs use `avoir`; être verbs use `être`.

items.french: ne + auxiliaire + pas + participe passé / Je n'ai pas mangé. / Elle n'est pas venue. / Nous ne sommes pas partis.
items.english: ne + auxiliary + pas + past participle / I did not eat. / She did not come. / We did not leave.
items.note: In the negative form, `ne ... pas` goes around the auxiliary.

items.french: -ER → -é: parler → parlé / manger → mangé / écouter → écouté / travailler → travaillé / visiter → visité
items.english: -ER becomes -é: spoken / eaten / listened / worked / visited
items.note: Regular -ER rule: drop `-er`, add `-é`.

items.french: -IR → -i: finir → fini / choisir → choisi / réussir → réussi / réfléchir → réfléchi / grandir → grandi
items.english: -IR becomes -i: finished / chosen / succeeded / thought / grown
items.note: Regular -IR rule: drop `-ir`, add `-i`.

items.french: Il est allé / Elle est allée / Ils sont allés / Elles sont allées / Marie et Sophie sont nées à Lyon.
items.english: He went / She went / They went / They went (all women) / Marie and Sophie were born in Lyon.
items.note: With `être`, the participle agrees in gender and number. No agreement needed with `avoir` at this level.

items.french: PRENDRE family: prendre → pris / apprendre → appris / comprendre → compris
items.english: taken / learned / understood
items.note: Useful irregular family to memorize together.

items.french: OUVRIR family: ouvrir → ouvert / offrir → offert / couvrir → couvert / découvrir → découvert
items.english: opened / offered / covered / discovered
items.note: Another common participle family pattern.

items.french: VENIR family: venir → venu / revenir → revenu / devenir → devenu
items.english: come / come back / become
items.note: Important because these often use `être`.

items.french: VOIR family: voir → vu / recevoir → reçu / apercevoir → aperçu
items.english: seen / received / noticed
items.note: Short irregular set worth grouping together.

items.french: U family: lire → lu / boire → bu / croire → cru / savoir → su / pouvoir → pu / devoir → dû / vouloir → voulu / courir → couru
items.english: read / drunk / believed / known / been able to / had to / wanted / run
items.note: Frequent irregular participles ending in `u`.

items.french: Core irregulars: être → été / avoir → eu / faire → fait / dire → dit / écrire → écrit / mettre → mis / conduire → conduit / vivre → vécu / résoudre → résolu / naître → né / mourir → mort / s'asseoir → assis
items.english: been / had / done / said / written / put / driven / lived / solved / born / died / sat down
items.note: Mixed high-value irregular participles.

items.french: devenir → devenu / revenir → revenu / monter → monté / rentrer → rentré / sortir → sorti / venir → venu / aller → allé / naître → né / descendre → descendu / entrer → entré / retourner → retourné / tomber → tombé / rester → resté / arriver → arrivé / mourir → mort / partir → parti
items.english: become / come back / gone up / come back home / gone out / come / gone / born / gone down / entered / returned / fallen / stayed / arrived / died / left
items.note: Classic memory list for common verbs that use `être` in the passé composé. Reflexive verbs also use `être`.

items.french: TEF/TCF essentials: être → été / avoir → eu / faire → fait / prendre → pris / comprendre → compris / dire → dit / écrire → écrit / voir → vu / lire → lu / boire → bu / recevoir → reçu / ouvrir → ouvert / offrir → offert / venir → venu / partir → parti / sortir → sorti
items.english: been / had / done / taken / understood / said / written / seen / read / drunk / received / opened / offered / come / left / gone out
items.note: Exam-relevant participles to know cold.

items.french: Nous avons allé au cinéma. → Nous sommes allés au cinéma.
items.english: We have went to the cinema. -> We went to the cinema.
items.note: Check the auxiliary first. `Aller` takes `être`, and with `être` you also need agreement.

title: Le mode impératif
summary: Use the imperative to give orders, advice, suggestions, and prohibitions.

items.french: Le mode impératif sert à donner un ordre, un conseil, une interdiction ou une suggestion.
items.english: The imperative is used to give an order, advice, a prohibition, or a suggestion.
items.note: There is no expressed subject, and it exists only in three persons: tu, nous, and vous.

items.french: L'impératif est généralement basé sur le présent de l'indicatif.
items.english: The imperative is generally based on the present tense of the indicative.
items.note: Most forms are built from the present-tense verb, with a few adjustments.

items.french: (Tu) Parle ! / (Nous) Parlons ! / (Vous) Parlez ! / (Tu) Mange ! / (Nous) Mangeons ! / (Vous) Mangez !
items.english: (You) Speak! / Let's speak! / Speak! / (You) Eat! / Let's eat! / Eat!
items.note: For most -ER verbs, the imperative matches the present tense, except `tu`, where the final `s` is dropped.

items.french: Vas-y ! / Manges-en !
items.english: Go there! / Eat some!
items.note: Exception: keep the `s` in the `tu` form when it is followed by `y` or `en`.

items.french: (Tu) Finis ! / (Nous) Finissons ! / (Vous) Finissez ! / (Tu) Prends ! / (Nous) Prenons ! / (Vous) Prenez ! / (Tu) Viens ! / (Nous) Venons ! / (Vous) Venez ! / (Tu) Lis ! / (Nous) Lisons ! / (Vous) Lisez !
items.english: (You) Finish! / Let's finish! / Finish! / (You) Take! / Let's take! / Take! / (You) Come! / Let's come! / Come! / (You) Read! / Let's read! / Read!
items.note: For most 2nd- and 3rd-group verbs, the imperative is identical to the present tense and keeps the `s` in the `tu` form.

items.french: Être → Sois ! / Soyons ! / Soyez ! / Avoir → Aie ! / Ayons ! / Ayez ! / Savoir → Sache ! / Sachons ! / Sachez !
items.english: Be! / Let's be! / Be! / Have! / Let's have! / Have! / Know! / Let's know! / Know!
items.note: These common verbs have irregular imperative forms and should be memorized directly.

items.french: Lève-toi ! / Levons-nous ! / Levez-vous ! / Dépêche-toi ! / Dépêchons-nous ! / Dépêchez-vous !
items.english: Get up! / Let's get up! / Get up! / Hurry up! / Let's hurry! / Hurry up!
items.note: In the affirmative imperative, reflexive pronouns become tonic pronouns (`toi`, `nous`, `vous`) and come after the verb with a hyphen.

items.french: Ne te lève pas ! / Ne nous levons pas ! / Ne vous levez pas ! / Ne te dépêche pas ! / Ne nous dépêchons pas ! / Ne vous dépêchez pas !
items.english: Don't get up! / Let's not get up! / Don't get up! / Don't hurry up! / Let's not hurry! / Don't hurry up!
items.note: In the negative imperative, the reflexive pronoun goes back before the verb between `ne` and the verb.

items.french: Ferme la porte ! / Venez avec moi ! / Faisons nos devoirs ! / Sois attentif en classe. / Réfléchissez avant de parler. / Mange équilibré. / Ne cours pas dans les couloirs ! / N'oubliez pas vos affaires ! / Ne t'inquiète pas.
items.english: Close the door! / Come with me! / Let's do our homework! / Be attentive in class. / Think before speaking. / Eat a balanced diet. / Don't run in the hallways! / Don't forget your things! / Don't worry.
items.note: These show the main uses of the imperative: order, advice, suggestion, and prohibition.

title: Les pronoms relatifs
summary: Use relative pronouns to connect two ideas and avoid repeating the same noun.

items.french: Les pronoms relatifs servent à relier deux phrases et à éviter la répétition d'un nom.
items.english: Relative pronouns are used to connect two sentences and avoid repeating a noun.
items.note: They introduce a relative clause.

items.french: J'ai un livre. Ce livre est intéressant. → J'ai un livre qui est intéressant.
items.english: I have a book. This book is interesting. -> I have a book that is interesting.
items.note: The repeated noun gets replaced by a relative pronoun.

items.french: qui / que / où / dont
items.english: who-that / that-which / where-when / of which-from which
items.note: These are the main relative pronouns to know at A2.

items.french: Qui remplace le sujet.
items.english: Qui replaces the subject.
items.note: Use `qui` when the replaced word is the subject of the verb.

items.french: L'homme qui parle est mon professeur. / Le chien qui court est rapide. / Voici l'élève qui a réussi.
items.english: The man who is speaking is my teacher. / The dog that is running is fast. / Here is the student who succeeded.
items.note: After `qui`, the verb follows directly because `qui` is the subject.

items.french: Que remplace le COD.
items.english: Que replaces the direct object.
items.note: Use `que` when the replaced word is the direct object. It is followed by a subject.

items.french: Le livre que je lis est intéressant. / La robe qu'elle porte est belle. / Le film que nous regardons est long.
items.english: The book that I am reading is interesting. / The dress that she is wearing is beautiful. / The film that we are watching is long.
items.note: `Que` becomes `qu'` before a vowel.

items.french: Où indique le lieu ou le temps.
items.english: Où indicates place or time.
items.note: Use `où` for where or when.

items.french: La ville où je vis est grande. / Le jour où je suis né est spécial. / Voici la maison où il habite.
items.english: The city where I live is big. / The day when I was born is special. / Here is the house where he lives.
items.note: `Où` can refer to a place or to a moment in time.

items.french: Dont remplace un complément introduit par `de`.
items.english: Dont replaces a complement introduced by `de`.
items.note: Useful with verbs like `parler de`, `avoir besoin de`, `se souvenir de`.

items.french: Le livre dont je parle est intéressant. / L'homme dont je me souviens est gentil. / C'est un sujet dont je parle.
items.english: The book that I am talking about is interesting. / The man I remember is kind. / It is a subject that I am talking about.
items.note: Ask yourself whether the original phrase uses `de`.

items.french: QUI = sujet / QUE = COD / OÙ = lieu ou temps / DONT = avec `de`
items.english: QUI = subject / QUE = direct object / OÙ = place or time / DONT = with `de`
items.note: This is the core distinction to memorize.

items.french: Le livre que je lis est intéressant. / La femme qui parle est ma mère. / La ville où je vais est Paris. / Le sujet dont je parle est difficile.
items.english: The book that I am reading is interesting. / The woman who is speaking is my mother. / The city where I am going is Paris. / The subject I am talking about is difficult.
items.note: Representative answers from the exercise patterns in the document.

items.french: Le livre qui je lis est intéressant. → Le livre que je lis est intéressant. / La femme que parle est gentille. → La femme qui parle est gentille. / La ville dont je vais est grande. → La ville où je vais est grande.
items.english: The book who I read is interesting. -> The book that I am reading is interesting. / The woman that speaks is kind. -> The woman who speaks is kind. / The city of which I go is big. -> The city where I go is big.
items.note: Typical confusion: `qui` for subject, `que` for COD, `où` for place, `dont` with `de`.

title: Les adverbes de fréquence
summary: Use frequency adverbs to show how often something happens and where to place them in a sentence.

items.french: Les adverbes de fréquence indiquent la régularité d'une action ou d'un événement.
items.english: Frequency adverbs show how regular an action or event is.
items.note: They answer questions like `À quelle fréquence ?` or `Combien de fois ?`.

items.french: toujours / constamment / fréquemment / régulièrement / habituellement / d'habitude / souvent
items.english: always / constantly / frequently / regularly / usually / usually / often
items.note: These express actions that happen very often.

items.french: parfois / quelquefois / de temps en temps / occasionnellement
items.english: sometimes / sometimes / from time to time / occasionally
items.note: These express actions that happen now and then.

items.french: rarement / presque jamais / jamais
items.english: rarely / almost never / never
items.note: These express actions that happen very little or not at all.

items.french: tous les jours / chaque jour / toutes les semaines / chaque semaine / tous les mois / chaque mois / tous les ans / chaque année / une fois par jour / deux fois par semaine
items.english: every day / each day / every week / each week / every month / each month / every year / each year / once a day / twice a week
items.note: These are fixed frequency expressions rather than single adverbs.

items.french: Il travaille souvent tard. / Nous allons toujours au marché le samedi. / Elle ne mange jamais de viande.
items.english: He often works late. / We always go to the market on Saturday. / She never eats meat.
items.note: With simple tenses, the frequency adverb usually comes after the verb.

items.french: J'ai souvent voyagé en France. / Elles sont toujours parties avant midi. / Il n'a jamais compris.
items.english: I have often traveled in France. / They have always left before noon. / He never understood.
items.note: With compound tenses, the frequency adverb usually goes between the auxiliary and the past participle.

items.french: De temps en temps, j'ai regardé ce film. / J'ai regardé ce film de temps en temps.
items.english: From time to time, I watched this film. / I watched this film from time to time.
items.note: Longer expressions can often go at the beginning or the end of the sentence.

items.french: Il faut toujours vérifier ses sources. / J'essaie de rarement manger dehors.
items.english: One must always check one's sources. / I try to rarely eat out.
items.note: With an infinitive, the adverb usually comes before the infinitive.

items.french: Souvent, il pleut en automne. / Je fais du sport tous les jours.
items.english: Often, it rains in autumn. / I do sports every day.
items.note: Some frequency adverbs or expressions can be moved to the beginning or end for emphasis.

items.french: Il ne vient jamais en avance. / Elle n'a jamais visité Paris.
items.english: He never comes early. / She has never visited Paris.
items.note: Negative frequency expressions like `ne ... jamais` place `ne` before the verb or auxiliary and `jamais` after it.

title: Le futur proche
summary: Use the futur proche to talk about something that is going to happen soon or a plan that is already decided.

items.french: Le futur proche sert à exprimer une action dans un avenir très immédiat ou une intention déjà fixée.
items.english: The near future is used to express an action in the very near future or an intention that is already decided.
items.note: It is very common in spoken French.

items.french: Sujet + aller (conjugué au présent) + verbe à l'infinitif + complément
items.english: Subject + aller (conjugated in the present) + infinitive verb + complement
items.note: Core structure: use the conjugated verb `aller` followed by an infinitive.

items.french: je vais / tu vas / il-elle-on va / nous allons / vous allez / ils-elles vont
items.english: I am going / you are going / he-she-one is going / we are going / you are going / they are going
items.note: You need the present-tense forms of `aller` to build the futur proche.

items.french: Je vais manger. / Tu vas dormir. / Elle va étudier. / Nous allons voyager. / Vous allez travailler. / Ils vont jouer.
items.english: I am going to eat. / You are going to sleep. / She is going to study. / We are going to travel. / You are going to work. / They are going to play.
items.note: These are the basic futur proche patterns.

items.french: Je vais manger au restaurant ce soir. / Tu vas voir un film demain. / Il va partir en vacances la semaine prochaine. / Nous allons acheter une voiture. / Vous allez finir votre travail bientôt. / Elles vont apprendre le français.
items.english: I am going to eat at the restaurant this evening. / You are going to see a movie tomorrow. / He is going to leave for vacation next week. / We are going to buy a car. / You are going to finish your work soon. / They are going to learn French.
items.note: Add time expressions or objects after the infinitive as usual.

items.french: Je vais sortir. / Je vais commencer le sport. / Nous allons déménager.
items.english: I am going to go out. / I am going to start sports. / We are going to move.
items.note: Main uses: imminent action, intention, and a decided plan.

items.french: Sujet + ne + aller + pas + infinitif / Je ne vais pas sortir ce soir. / Tu ne vas pas venir à mon anniversaire. / Ruben ne va pas travailler pendant 5 jours.
items.english: Subject + ne + aller + pas + infinitive / I am not going to go out this evening. / You are not going to come to my birthday. / Ruben is not going to work for 5 days.
items.note: The negation wraps around the conjugated form of `aller`.

items.french: Tu vas venir ? / Est-ce que tu vas venir ? / Vas-tu venir ?
items.english: Are you going to come? / Are you going to come? / Are you going to come?
items.note: You can ask questions with intonation, `est-ce que`, or inversion.

items.french: Je vais manger ✔ / Je vais mange ✘
items.english: I am going to eat ✔ / I am going to eats ✘
items.note: Use the infinitive after `aller`, not another conjugated verb.

items.french: Je vais / Tu vas / Il va / Nous allons / Vous allez / Ils vont
items.english: I am going / You are going / He is going / We are going / You are going / They are going
items.note: Do not forget to conjugate `aller` correctly.

title: Les articles partitifs
summary: Use partitive articles for an unspecified quantity, especially with things that are not counted individually.

items.french: Les articles partitifs servent à parler d'une quantité indéterminée, c'est-à-dire une partie d'un tout qu'on ne peut pas compter.
items.english: Partitive articles are used to talk about an unspecified quantity, that is, part of a whole that cannot be counted.
items.note: In English this often corresponds to `some` or no article at all.

items.french: Je mange du pain. / Elle boit de l'eau.
items.english: I eat some bread. / She drinks some water.
items.note: These are basic examples of an unspecified quantity.

items.french: du / de la / de l' / des
items.english: some masculine singular / some feminine singular / some before vowel or silent h / some plural
items.note: These are the four main partitive forms.

items.french: du fromage / de la soupe / de l'huile / des légumes
items.english: some cheese / some soup / some oil / some vegetables
items.note: Use `du` for masculine singular, `de la` for feminine singular, `de l'` before vowel or silent h, and `des` for plural.

items.french: du lait / de la farine / de l'argent
items.english: some milk / some flour / some money
items.note: Use partitive articles with uncountable nouns.

items.french: Je veux du café. / Elle mange de la viande.
items.english: I want some coffee. / She eats some meat.
items.note: Use them to express a vague or unspecified quantity.

items.french: beaucoup de / un kilo de / un peu de
items.english: a lot of / a kilo of / a little of
items.note: With a precise quantity expression, the partitive is replaced by `de`.

items.french: Je bois beaucoup d'eau. / Il achète un kilo de pommes.
items.english: I drink a lot of water. / He buys a kilo of apples.
items.note: After these expressions, use `de` or `d'`.

items.french: le pain / un pain / du pain
items.english: the bread / a loaf of bread / some bread
items.note: Definite article = specific thing, indefinite article = countable thing, partitive article = unspecified quantity.

items.french: Peut-on compter ? / du sucre, de l'eau / une pomme, des livres
items.english: Can it be counted? / some sugar, some water / an apple, some books
items.note: If the answer is no, use a partitive. If yes, use `un`, `une`, or `des`.

items.french: Je mange du chocolat. / Elle prépare de la soupe. / Nous buvons de l'eau. / Ils achètent des fruits. / Je ne veux pas de sucre.
items.english: I eat some chocolate. / She makes some soup. / We drink some water. / They buy some fruit. / I do not want any sugar.
items.note: Useful mixed examples of affirmative and negative use.

items.french: du / de la / de l' / des → de / d'
items.english: partitive articles become `de` or `d'` in negation
items.note: At the negative form, partitive articles usually change to `de` or `d'`.

items.french: Sujet + ne + verbe + pas + de / d' + nom
items.english: Subject + ne + verb + pas + de / d' + noun
items.note: This is the basic negative structure.

items.french: Je mange du pain → Je ne mange pas de pain. / Elle boit de la soupe → Elle ne boit pas de soupe. / Il prend de l'eau → Il ne prend pas d'eau. / Nous achetons des fruits → Nous n'achetons pas de fruits.
items.english: I eat some bread -> I do not eat any bread. / She drinks some soup -> She does not drink any soup. / He takes some water -> He does not take any water. / We buy some fruit -> We do not buy any fruit.
items.note: Negation often expresses zero quantity, so use the neutral `de` form.

items.french: C'est du fromage → Ce n'est pas du fromage. / C'est de la soupe → Ce n'est pas de la soupe.
items.english: It is some cheese -> It is not some cheese. / It is some soup -> It is not some soup.
items.note: With the verb `être`, the partitive article does not change in the negative.

items.french: Je ne bois jamais de café. / Elle ne mange plus de légumes. / Je ne bois pas d'eau.
items.english: I never drink coffee. / She no longer eats vegetables. / I do not drink water.
items.note: The same `de / d'` rule applies with `ne ... jamais` and `ne ... plus`. Before a vowel or silent h, use `d'`.

title: Les pronoms possessifs
summary: Use possessive pronouns to replace a noun and show possession without repeating the noun.

items.french: Les pronoms possessifs remplacent un nom pour éviter la répétition et indiquent la possession.
items.english: Possessive pronouns replace a noun to avoid repetition and show possession.
items.note: They replace `possessive adjective + noun`.

items.french: C'est mon livre. → C'est le mien. / Voici ta voiture. → Voici la tienne.
items.english: It is my book. -> It is mine. / Here is your car. -> Here is yours.
items.note: Basic pattern: the noun disappears and the possessive pronoun replaces it.

items.french: le mien / le tien / le sien / le nôtre / le vôtre / le leur
items.english: mine / yours / his-hers / ours / yours / theirs
items.note: Masculine singular forms.

items.french: la mienne / la tienne / la sienne / la nôtre / la vôtre / la leur
items.english: mine / yours / hers-his / ours / yours / theirs
items.note: Feminine singular forms.

items.french: les miens / les tiens / les siens / les nôtres / les vôtres / les leurs
items.english: mine / yours / his-hers / ours / yours / theirs
items.note: Masculine plural or mixed plural forms.

items.french: les miennes / les tiennes / les siennes / les nôtres / les vôtres / les leurs
items.english: mine / yours / hers-his / ours / yours / theirs
items.note: Feminine plural forms.

items.french: mon stylo → le mien / ta maison → la tienne / nos amis → les nôtres
items.english: my pen -> mine / your house -> yours / our friends -> ours
items.note: Structure: `possessive adjective + noun` becomes `possessive pronoun`.

items.french: Ton sac est noir. Le mien est rouge. / Sa voiture est rapide. La sienne est neuve.
items.english: Your bag is black. Mine is red. / Her car is fast. Hers is new.
items.note: The possessive pronoun agrees with the noun replaced, not with the owner.

items.french: Vos livres sont ici. Les nôtres sont là-bas. / Mes chaussures sont sales. Les tiennes sont propres.
items.english: Your books are here. Ours are over there. / My shoes are dirty. Yours are clean.
items.note: Check both gender and number of the noun being replaced.

items.french: mon livre / ta voiture / nos amis → le mien / la tienne / les nôtres
items.english: my book / your car / our friends -> mine / yours / ours
items.note: Possessive adjectives go before a noun. Possessive pronouns replace the noun.

items.french: Ce sac est le mien. / Cette robe est la tienne. / Ces clés sont les leurs. / Notre appartement est plus grand que le vôtre. / Son vélo est cassé ; le mien fonctionne bien.
items.english: This bag is mine. / This dress is yours. / These keys are theirs. / Our apartment is bigger than yours. / His bike is broken; mine works well.
items.note: Useful sentence-level examples.

items.french: À qui est cette voiture ? — C'est la mienne. / Et ces livres ? — Ce sont les tiens. / Où sont leurs sacs ? — Les leurs sont dans la voiture.
items.english: Whose car is this? — It's mine. / And these books? — They are yours. / Where are their bags? — Theirs are in the car.
items.note: Common conversational use.

items.french: le mienne ✘ → la mienne ✔ / les notre ✘ → les nôtres ✔
items.english: wrong masculine-feminine form / missing accent and plural form
items.note: Watch gender agreement and forms like `nôtre` and `vôtre`.

items.french: livre → le mien / table → la mienne / livres → les miens / tables → les miennes
items.english: book -> mine / table -> mine / books -> mine / tables -> mine
items.note: Check the noun replaced first: masculine singular, feminine singular, masculine plural, feminine plural.

title: Le passé récent
summary: Use the passé récent to talk about something that happened just before the present moment.

items.french: Le passé récent exprime une action terminée juste avant le moment où l'on parle.
items.english: The recent past expresses an action that finished just before the moment of speaking.
items.note: It emphasizes the closeness in time between the action and the present.

items.french: Je viens de finir mon travail.
items.english: I have just finished my work.
items.note: This means the action happened only a moment ago.

items.french: Sujet + venir (au présent) + de + verbe à l'infinitif + complément
items.english: Subject + venir (in the present) + de + infinitive verb + complement
items.note: This is the basic formula for the passé récent.

items.french: Je viens de manger des croissants.
items.english: I have just eaten croissants.
items.note: This is a verbal periphrasis: several words form one time meaning.

items.french: Je = sujet / viens = verbe venir conjugué / de = préposition / manger = infinitif
items.english: Je = subject / viens = conjugated verb venir / de = preposition / manger = infinitive
items.note: The real action is in the infinitive, not in `venir`.

items.french: je viens / tu viens / il-elle vient / nous venons / vous venez / ils-elles viennent
items.english: I come / you come / he-she comes / we come / you come / they come
items.note: You need the present-tense forms of `venir` for the basic passé récent.

items.french: Je viens de partir. / Je venais de partir quand il est arrivé. / Je viendrai de finir quand tu arriveras.
items.english: I have just left. / I had just left when he arrived. / I will have just finished when you arrive.
items.note: The tense of `venir` changes depending on the time frame: present, imperfect, or future.

items.french: Elle venait de sortir quand il a appelé. / Nous venions de commencer le cours.
items.english: She had just gone out when he called. / We had just started the lesson.
items.note: Use `venir` in the imperfect to express something that had just happened before another past action.

items.french: Je viens de le voir. / Elle vient de m'appeler. / Nous venons de leur parler.
items.english: I have just seen him-it. / She has just called me. / We have just spoken to them.
items.note: Pronouns go before the infinitive, not before `venir`.

items.french: Je le viens de voir ✘ / Je viens de le voir ✔
items.english: Wrong pronoun position ✘ / Correct pronoun position ✔
items.note: This is a very common mistake.

items.french: Je viens de me lever. / Elle vient de se coucher. / Ils viennent de s'habiller.
items.english: I have just gotten up. / She has just gone to bed. / They have just gotten dressed.
items.note: With reflexive verbs, keep the reflexive pronoun before the infinitive.

items.french: Sujet + ne + venir + pas + de + infinitif
items.english: Subject + ne + venir + pas + de + infinitive
items.note: Basic negative structure of the passé récent.

items.french: Je ne viens pas de manger. / Elle ne vient pas de partir. / Je ne viens jamais de manger. / Il ne vient plus de travailler. / Elle ne vient pas encore de finir.
items.english: I have not just eaten. / She has not just left. / I have never just eaten. / He no longer has just worked. / She has not finished just yet.
items.note: Other negative words like `jamais`, `plus`, and `pas encore` can also be used.

items.french: Tu viens de finir ? / Est-ce que tu viens de finir ? / Viens-tu de finir ?
items.english: Did you just finish? / Did you just finish? / Did you just finish?
items.note: You can ask a question with intonation, `est-ce que`, or inversion.

items.french: Il vient de partir. / Tu viens de dire quoi ? / Je viens de terminer !
items.english: He has just left. / What did you just say? / I have just finished!
items.note: The passé récent can express immediacy, surprise, or insistence on a result.

items.french: Je viens de manger / J'ai mangé / Je mangeais / Je venais de manger
items.english: I have just eaten / I ate / I was eating / I had just eaten
items.note: Use the passé récent when you want to stress that the action happened very recently.

items.french: juste / à l'instant / tout juste / il y a une seconde / Je viens juste de finir. / Elle vient à l'instant de partir.
items.english: just / just now / just barely / a second ago / I have just finished. / She has just left this very moment.
items.note: These expressions often appear with the passé récent.

items.french: Je viens de mangé ✘ → Je viens de manger ✔ / Je viens manger ✘ → Je viens de manger ✔ / Je le viens de voir ✘ → Je viens de le voir ✔
items.english: Wrong past participle-like form ✘ / Correct infinitive ✔ / Missing `de` ✘ / Correct structure ✔ / Wrong pronoun position ✘ / Correct pronoun position ✔
items.note: Three common errors: using a past participle, forgetting `de`, or putting the pronoun in the wrong place.

items.french: Je viens de comprendre pourquoi il est parti. / Elle vient de recevoir un message important. / Nous venons de terminer un projet difficile. / Ils viennent de découvrir la vérité.
items.english: I have just understood why he left. / She has just received an important message. / We have just finished a difficult project. / They have just discovered the truth.
items.note: Longer examples show how natural the structure is in real sentences.

title: L'imparfait
summary: Use the imparfait for ongoing past actions, repeated habits, states, descriptions, and background situations.

items.french: L'imparfait décrit des actions en cours dans le passé, des habitudes, des états ou le contexte.
items.english: The imperfect describes ongoing past actions, habits, states, or background context.
items.note: It is used when the past action is not seen as completed at one precise moment.

items.french: actions en cours / habitudes dans le passé / états physiques ou émotionnels / descriptions ou arrière-plan / heure ou âge dans le passé
items.english: ongoing actions / habits in the past / physical or emotional states / descriptions or background / time or age in the past
items.note: These are the main situations where the imparfait is used.

items.french: Quand j'étais petit, je mangeais souvent des glaces. / Le dimanche, nous allions toujours à l'église. / Elle travaillait six jours par semaine. / Les enfants jouaient dans le parc chaque après-midi. / Autrefois, les gens voyageaient en train.
items.english: When I was little, I often ate ice cream. / On Sundays, we always went to church. / She used to work six days a week. / The children played in the park every afternoon. / In the past, people traveled by train.
items.note: Use the imparfait for repeated or usual actions in the past.

items.french: J'étais très fatigué ce jour-là. / Nous avions peur du noir. / Elle aimait ce garçon depuis longtemps. / Il faisait beau et chaud. / Les enfants étaient malades.
items.english: I was very tired that day. / We were afraid of the dark. / She had loved that boy for a long time. / The weather was nice and warm. / The children were sick.
items.note: Use the imparfait for states, feelings, weather, and longer emotional situations.

items.french: La maison était grande et blanche. / Les rues étaient désertes. / Il y avait beaucoup de monde au restaurant. / Paris semblait différent à cette époque. / Les arbres avaient des feuilles jaunes.
items.english: The house was large and white. / The streets were deserted. / There were many people at the restaurant. / Paris seemed different at that time. / The trees had yellow leaves.
items.note: Use the imparfait to set the scene or describe the background of a story.

items.french: Je prenais mon petit-déjeuner quand le téléphone a sonné. / Ils regardaient la télévision quand je suis arrivé. / Pendant qu'elle dormait, quelqu'un est entré dans la maison.
items.english: I was having breakfast when the phone rang. / They were watching television when I arrived. / While she was sleeping, someone entered the house.
items.note: The action in progress is in the imparfait. The interrupting completed action is often in the passé composé.

items.french: Prenez la forme `nous` du présent, enlevez `-ons`, puis ajoutez : -ais, -ais, -ait, -ions, -iez, -aient.
items.english: Take the `nous` form of the present tense, remove `-ons`, then add: -ais, -ais, -ait, -ions, -iez, -aient.
items.note: This is the standard way to form the imparfait.

items.french: parler → nous parlons → parl- → je parlais / tu parlais / il-elle parlait / nous parlions / vous parliez / ils-elles parlaient
items.english: parler -> we speak -> parl- -> I was speaking / you were speaking / he-she was speaking / we were speaking / you were speaking / they were speaking
items.note: Use the `nous` stem and then add the imparfait endings.

items.french: être → j'étais / tu étais / il-elle était / nous étions / vous étiez / ils-elles étaient
items.english: to be -> I was / you were / he-she was / we were / you were / they were
items.note: `Être` is the main irregular verb in the imparfait.

items.french: Quand j'étais petit, je mangeais des glaces tous les jours. / Il faisait beau ce jour-là. / Je prenais mon petit-déjeuner quand le téléphone a sonné. / La maison était grande et avait un beau jardin.
items.english: When I was little, I used to eat ice cream every day. / The weather was nice that day. / I was having breakfast when the phone rang. / The house was large and had a beautiful garden.
items.note: These examples cover habit, state, interrupted action, and description.

title: Comparatives and superlatives
summary: Use comparatives to compare two things and superlatives to show the highest or lowest degree.

items.french: plus... que / aussi... que / moins... que
items.english: more... than / as... as / less... than
items.note: These are the three main comparative patterns: superiority, equality, and inferiority.

items.french: Pierre est plus grand que Paul. / Cette maison est aussi belle que l'autre. / Ce film est moins intéressant que celui-là.
items.english: Pierre is taller than Paul. / This house is as beautiful as the other one. / This movie is less interesting than that one.
items.note: Basic structure: `plus`, `aussi`, or `moins` + adjective + `que`.

items.french: Elle est plus intelligente que lui. / Ils sont moins fatigués que nous.
items.english: She is more intelligent than him. / They are less tired than us.
items.note: The adjective still agrees with the subject in gender and number.

items.french: plus de / autant de / moins de + nom + que
items.english: more of / as many-much as / less of + noun + than
items.note: Use these forms when comparing quantities of nouns.

items.french: J'ai plus de livres que toi. / Elle a autant d'amis que lui. / Il a moins d'argent que moi. / Il a plus d'expérience que moi.
items.english: I have more books than you. / She has as many friends as him. / He has less money than me. / He has more experience than me.
items.note: Use `d'` instead of `de` before a vowel.

items.french: bon → meilleur / bien → mieux / mauvais → pire
items.english: good → better / well → better / bad → worse
items.note: These are the main irregular comparative forms.

items.french: Ce restaurant est meilleur que l'autre. / Elle parle français mieux que moi. / Ce film est pire que le premier.
items.english: This restaurant is better than the other one. / She speaks French better than me. / This movie is worse than the first one.
items.note: `Plus mauvais que` is also acceptable for `worse`.

items.french: le / la / les + plus / moins + adjectif + de / du / des / d'
items.english: the + most / least + adjective + of / in
items.note: Superlatives express the highest or lowest degree.

items.french: C'est le plus grand bâtiment de la ville. / Elle est la plus intelligente de sa classe. / Ce sont les moins chers des produits.
items.english: It is the tallest building in the city. / She is the most intelligent in her class. / These are the least expensive of the products.
items.note: Use the definite article before the superlative.

items.french: C'est la plus belle maison. / C'est la maison la plus belle. / Le plus grand garçon / La plus grande fille / Les plus grands garçons / Les plus grandes filles
items.english: It is the most beautiful house. / It is the house that is the most beautiful. / The tallest boy / The tallest girl / The tallest boys / The tallest girls
items.note: Superlatives can go before or after the noun, and the adjective agrees in gender and number.

items.french: bon → le meilleur / la meilleure / les meilleur(e)s / bien → le mieux / mauvais → le pire / la pire / les pires
items.english: good → the best / the best feminine / the best plural / well → the best / bad → the worst / the worst feminine / the worst plural
items.note: These are the main irregular superlative forms.

items.french: C'est le meilleur restaurant de la ville. / C'est la meilleure solution. / Elle danse le mieux de tous. / C'est le pire film que j'ai vu.
items.english: It is the best restaurant in the city. / It is the best solution. / She dances the best of everyone. / It is the worst film I have seen.
items.note: `Le plus mauvais` is also acceptable for `the worst`.
